skill.name: ai-avatar-video
skill.description: Create AI avatar, talking head, lipsync, and dubbing videos through inference.sh belt CLI workflows.
skill.summary: Generate avatar presenter videos from portraits, scripts, or audio using inference.sh belt commands.
skill.author: halt-catch-fire

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified extensive external command and network usage, but the Ruby backtick labels and weak cryptography warning are false positives in context. The skill intentionally documents belt CLI calls to remote inference.sh video, TTS, and lipsync services, so publication is reasonable with a privacy and third-party processing warning.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution for Remote Video Generation
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs users to run belt app commands. This is expected for the skill, but it executes a local CLI that submits jobs to external media-generation services, so users should review inputs before running commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct belt command examples are present throughout the skill. The commands match the stated purpose, but they still cause external processing through a CLI.

medium_findings.title: User Media and Scripts Sent to Third-Party Services
medium_findings.description: The documented workflows pass portrait URLs, audio URLs, video URLs, and spoken scripts to remote inference.sh applications. This is legitimate functionality, but it can expose personal images, voices, marketing scripts, or unreleased media to a third party.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly include remote image, audio, and video URL fields plus text scripts. The data sharing is intended, but privacy impact is clear.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Weak Cryptography Static Finding
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography alert at line 3 is a false positive. The line is frontmatter text describing avatar video capabilities, and no cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic code is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 contains descriptive metadata only. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or related weak cryptographic use in the inspected skill file.

low_findings.title: Static Command Detector Misclassified Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer labeled many Markdown command examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The file is an instructional Markdown skill, and the detected backticks are code formatting or fenced shell examples rather than embedded executable Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is Markdown, not Ruby source. The detected backticks appear in prose and fenced command examples, although the commands themselves remain relevant operational guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Permission Restricted to belt Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash access for belt commands. This narrows the command surface, but it still permits running an external CLI that can authenticate, submit jobs, and contact remote services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools frontmatter and first CLI workflow are explicit. The permission is scoped, but the permitted command has networked side effects.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded Remote Service URLs in Examples and Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes hardcoded inference.sh documentation links and placeholder media URLs. These support the intended workflow, but they confirm reliance on external network services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Remote URLs are visible in the documentation. They appear benign and relevant, but they establish a third-party dependency users should understand.

content.value_statement: Producing talking head videos often requires separate image, voice, and lipsync tools. This skill shows how to use inference.sh belt commands to create avatar videos, UGC clips, dubbing workflows, and presenter content.
actual_capabilities: Shows belt CLI commands for P-Video-Avatar, OmniHuman, Fabric, PixVerse, and LatentSync workflows.
Explains how to create avatar videos from a portrait image and text script.
Documents audio-driven avatar and lipsync flows using external audio URLs.
Provides workflows for generating portraits, speech, avatar video, and localized dubbing outputs.
Compares supported avatar models by speed, cost, use case, and built-in TTS support.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh belt CLI and an authenticated account.
Sends image, audio, video, and script inputs to third-party inference services.
Does not include local model execution or offline video generation.
Output quality depends on source portrait quality, voice clarity, and selected model behavior.

use_cases.title: Launch Product Presenter Clips
use_cases.description: Create short talking head videos from a product script and a generated or provided presenter portrait.

use_cases.title: Localize Training Videos
use_cases.description: Transcribe, translate, generate new speech, and lipsync existing videos for another language.

use_cases.title: Prototype Game Character Dialogue
use_cases.description: Turn character portraits and dialogue lines into quick avatar videos for NPC concepts or story review.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Avatar Video
prompt_templates.scenario: Create one presenter video from a portrait and a short script.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ai-avatar-video skill to create a talking head video from this portrait URL and script. Recommend the simplest model and explain the required belt command.

prompt_templates.title: UGC Ad Variant
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a social video with a casual testimonial style.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this product message to plan a UGC-style avatar video. Choose a voice, portrait style, video prompt, and resolution for a vertical ad.

prompt_templates.title: Dubbing Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert an existing video into another spoken language.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to outline a dubbing workflow for this video URL. Include transcription, translation, TTS, and lipsync steps.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Model Selection
prompt_templates.scenario: Compare avatar models for quality, cost, and workflow complexity.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare P-Video-Avatar, OmniHuman, Fabric, and PixVerse for this project. Recommend one model and list the tradeoffs before giving commands.

output_examples.input: A marketer wants a 20-second vertical product demo from a presenter image and a short launch script.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends P-Video-Avatar, selects a built-in voice, suggests 1080p output, and provides a belt command plan.

output_examples.input: An educator has an English training video and needs a Spanish talking head version.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines transcription, translation, Spanish speech generation, and lipsync steps using inference.sh apps.

output_examples.input: A game designer wants dialogue clips for three NPC portraits.
output_examples.output: The skill suggests batch avatar generation with different voices, character tone prompts, and consistent video settings.

best_practices: Use front-facing portraits with clear lighting and minimal occlusion.
Review all image, audio, video, and script inputs before sending them to remote services.
Start with P-Video-Avatar unless a workflow specifically needs multi-character or advanced lipsync behavior.
anti_patterns: Do not submit private faces, voices, or unreleased scripts without permission.
Do not assume generated speech or lipsync will match brand safety requirements without review.
Do not use low-quality portraits or noisy audio when realistic output is required.

faq.question: Does this skill generate videos locally?
faq.answer: No. It documents belt CLI workflows that submit jobs to inference.sh apps.

faq.question: Which model should most users start with?
faq.answer: The skill recommends P-Video-Avatar because it includes built-in TTS and simpler setup.

faq.question: Can it use my own audio file?
faq.answer: Yes. Several examples use external audio URLs for audio-driven avatars and lipsync workflows.

faq.question: Can it help with video localization?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a workflow for transcription, translation, TTS, and lipsync.

faq.question: What data may leave my environment?
faq.answer: Portrait URLs, audio URLs, video URLs, text scripts, and generated media may be processed by third-party services.

faq.question: Does it require another CLI skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill points users to install and authenticate the belt CLI before running examples.
